item.title: Impact of Announced Wait Time Information on Emergency Department Overcrowding Mitigation: A Simulation Study
Patient Sensitivity to Emergency Department Waiting Time Announcements
item.description:
What this paper is about

Many emergency departments publish expected waiting times to help patients choose where to seek care. Using over 1.3 million patient visits to Hong Kong public EDs, this study estimates how many patients actually respond to these announcements and how sensitive they are to the reported times. The findings inform the design of announcement systems that can reduce waiting times and the number of patients who leave without being seen.

item.title: Time-Varying Physician Productivity and Implications for Emergency Department Modeling and Staffing
Physician Scheduling in Case Managers Style Emergency Departments: Machine Learning-Aided Solution Approaches
Service-level Computation in Time-Varying Queueing System with Priorities: Application to Physician Staffing in the Emergency Department
Exact Algorithm and Machine Learning-Based Heuristic for the Stochastic Lot Streaming and Scheduling Problem
Assigning Priorities (or not) in Service Systems with Nonlinear Waiting Costs
The Impact of Emergency Department Crowding on Admission Decisions and Patient Outcomes
Scheduling of Physicians with Time-Varying Productivity Levels in Emergency Departments
On Scheduling a Two-Class Queue with Concave Waiting Cost
Priority Scheduling When Job Type Information Is Not Free
Allocation of Intensive Care Unit Beds in Periods of High Demand
item.description:
What this paper is about

When an intensive care unit (ICU) runs out of beds during a surge, hospitals must decide whether to admit a new patient to the ICU or transfer an existing patient to a general ward. This paper develops a Markov-chain model of patient health evolution and proposes admission/transfer policies that minimize the long-run mortality rate.
